rules.rule_name: There is/are - Existence Across All Tenses
rules.explanation_en: Complete reference for expressing existence in all English tenses, including perfect tenses and modal perfects (should/could/would/might have). Essential for describing what exists now, existed before, or will exist in the future.
rules.explanation_es: Referencia completa para expresar existencia en todos los tiempos del inglés, incluyendo tiempos perfectos y modales perfectos (should/could/would/might have). Esencial para describir lo que existe ahora, existió antes, o existirá en el futuro.
examples: Present: There is a book on the table. / There are many students here.
Past: There was a party last night. / There were problems yesterday.
Future: There will be consequences. / There will be many opportunities.
Present Perfect: There has been an accident. / There have been complaints.
Past Perfect: There had been warnings before the storm.
Future Perfect: By 2030, there will have been many changes.
Would Have: If you had come earlier, there would have been food.
Should Have: There should have been more security at the event.
Could Have: There could have been serious injuries in that accident.
Might Have: There might have been a misunderstanding.
The dog (S) quickly (Adv) chased (V) the ball (O) in the park (PP).

rules.explanation_en: Some words naturally go together. 'Pay' goes with 'attention'.
rules.explanation_es: Algunas palabras van juntas naturalmente. 'Pay' va con 'attention'.
examples: Pay attention to the teacher.

rules.explanation_en: Used for life experiences when the exact time doesn't matter.
rules.explanation_es: Se usa para experiencias de vida cuando el tiempo exacto no importa.
examples: Have you ever traveled to Asia?
She has never driven a car.

rules.explanation_en: Used for real possibilities in the future.
rules.explanation_es: Se usa para posibilidades reales en el futuro.
examples: If it rains, I will stay inside.

rules.explanation_en: Used to compare HOW an action is done.
rules.explanation_es: Se usa para comparar CÓMO se hace una acción.
examples: He speaks English more fluently than before.

rules.explanation_en: When an action acts as a noun.
rules.explanation_es: Cuando una acción funciona como un sustantivo.
examples: Playing soccer is fun.
I hardly ever drink soda.

rules.meaning_es: Expresiones cuyo significado NO es literal.
examples: It's raining cats and dogs.

rules.explanation_en: Used for past regrets or criticism.
rules.explanation_es: Usado para arrepentimientos o críticas del pasado.
examples: We should have left earlier.

rules.explanation_en: Pronouns go in the middle for separable phrasal verbs.
rules.explanation_es: Los pronombres van en medio en phrasal verbs separables.
examples: Pick me up at 8.
It's hot today, isn't it?
Such a good movie!
This movie is so good!

rules.meaning_en: To show how long something will have been happening.
rules.meaning_es: Para mostrar cuánto tiempo habrá estado ocurriendo algo.
examples: Next June, I will have been working here for 10 years.

rules.meaning_en: Shows one past action happened before another.
rules.meaning_es: Muestra que una acción pasada ocurrió antes que otra.
examples: Having finished the work, he went home.
I need to study.
I enjoy studying.

rules.rule_name: Used to / Be used to / Get used to
examples: I used to smoke.
I'm used to waking up early.
She's getting used to her new job.

structure.ing_form: Describes the action or state causing emotion (active, continuous, or causative)
structure.ed_form: Describes the feeling experienced (passive or received)

rules.explanation_en: -ING describes things or situations. -ED describes feelings or reactions.
rules.explanation_es: -ING describe cosas o situaciones. -ED describe sentimientos o reacciones.
examples: The movie was exciting. (The movie caused excitement)
I was excited by the movie. (I experienced excitement)

rules.explanation_en: Indicates regret or criticism about something that didn't happen.
rules.explanation_es: Indica arrepentimiento o crítica sobre algo que no sucedió.
examples: You should have called me earlier.

rules.rule_name: You Gotta vs You've Got vs You Have

structure.you_have: Formal - Standard English (You have to go)

rules.explanation_en: Three ways to express obligation or necessity with different levels of formality. 'You have' is standard formal English. 'You've got' is informal but acceptable in conversation. 'You gotta' is very informal slang (contraction of 'got to') used in casual speech but should be avoided in writing.
rules.explanation_es: Tres formas de expresar obligación o necesidad con diferentes niveles de formalidad. 'You have' es inglés formal estándar. 'You've got' es informal pero aceptable en conversación. 'You gotta' es argot muy informal (contracción de 'got to') usado en habla casual pero debe evitarse en escritura.
examples: Formal: You have to finish this report by Friday.
Informal: You've got to see this movie, it's amazing!
Very Informal/Slang: You gotta try this pizza! (spoken only)
Possession (have): You have a beautiful house.
Possession (have got): You've got a beautiful house.
Obligation: You have to be there at 9 AM.
Obligation: You've got to be there at 9 AM.
Slang: You gotta be kidding me! (You must be joking)
rules.note: ⚠️ 'You gotta' should NOT be used in formal writing, academic papers, or professional communication. Use 'you have to' or 'you must' instead.

structure.can: Ability, Permission, Possibility (I can swim / Can I go? / It can be difficult)
structure.must: Strong Obligation, Necessity, Logical Deduction (You must study / It must be true)
structure.might: Possibility, Uncertainty, Permission (formal) (It might rain / Might I ask?)

rules.explanation_en: Modal verbs that express different levels of ability, obligation, and possibility. 'Can' shows ability or asks permission. 'Must' indicates strong obligation or certainty. 'Might' expresses possibility or uncertainty (weaker than 'may').
rules.explanation_es: Verbos modales que expresan diferentes niveles de habilidad, obligación y posibilidad. 'Can' muestra habilidad o pide permiso. 'Must' indica obligación fuerte o certeza. 'Might' expresa posibilidad o incertidumbre (más débil que 'may').
examples: Ability: I can speak three languages.
Permission: Can I use your phone?
Possibility: Learning English can be challenging.
Strong Obligation: You must wear a seatbelt.
Necessity: We must finish this today.
Logical Deduction: She must be tired after working all day.
Possibility (uncertain): It might rain tomorrow.
Polite Suggestion: You might want to check your email.
Formal Permission: Might I suggest an alternative?
Negative: You can't park here. / You must not smoke here. / It might not work.
Past: I could swim when I was young. / I had to study. / It might have been true.
rules.note: 💡 Tip: 'Can' is more common than 'may' for permission in modern English. 'Must' is stronger than 'should'. 'Might' is less certain than 'may'.

rules.rule_name: We Gotta vs We Got vs We Have vs We've Got

structure.we_have: Formal - Standard English (We have a problem / We have to go)
structure.we_ve_got: Informal - Conversational (We've got a problem / We've got to go)
structure.we_got: Informal - Possession only, NOT obligation (We got a new car - past tense)
structure.we_gotta: Very Informal - Slang/Spoken obligation only (We gotta go - spoken only)

rules.explanation_en: Four different ways to express possession or obligation with varying formality levels. 'We have' is standard formal English for both possession and obligation. 'We've got' is informal but grammatically correct for both. 'We got' is ONLY for possession (often past tense) and NEVER for obligation. 'We gotta' is very informal slang for obligation only (contraction of 'got to'), used in casual speech but should be avoided in writing.
rules.explanation_es: Cuatro formas diferentes de expresar posesión u obligación con diferentes niveles de formalidad. 'We have' es inglés formal estándar para posesión y obligación. 'We've got' es informal pero gramaticalmente correcto para ambos. 'We got' es SOLO para posesión (a menudo pasado) y NUNCA para obligación. 'We gotta' es argot muy informal solo para obligación (contracción de 'got to'), usado en habla casual pero debe evitarse en escritura.
examples: Possession (formal): We have three children.
Possession (informal): We've got three children.
Possession (past): We got a new house last year.
Obligation (formal): We have to leave now.
Obligation (informal): We've got to leave now.
Obligation (slang): We gotta go! (spoken only)
WRONG: We got to leave now. ❌ (Use 'We have to' or 'We've got to')
RIGHT: We got a promotion. ✅ (past tense possession)
WRONG: We gotta new car. ❌ (Use 'We got' or 'We have')
Emphasis: We've got to finish this today!
Present perfect: We've got everything we need.
Common mistake: Don't confuse 'we got' (past possession) with 'we've got to' (obligation)
rules.note: ⚠️ IMPORTANT: 'We got' = past possession ONLY. For obligation, use 'we have to' (formal), 'we've got to' (informal), or 'we gotta' (slang/spoken). Never use 'we got to' for obligation - it's grammatically incorrect.

structure.questions: Have you ever...? / Did you ever...? (asking about experience)
structure.negatives: I don't ever... / I never... (emphasizing negative)
structure.comparatives: better than ever / more than ever (intensifier)
structure.conditionals: If you ever need help... (at any time)
structure.superlatives: the best ever / the fastest ever (of all time)
structure.emphasis: Why ever...? / What ever...? / How ever...? (strong questioning)

rules.explanation_en: 'Ever' has multiple uses in English: 1) In questions about life experiences (Have you ever...?), 2) With negatives for emphasis (don't ever, never ever), 3) In comparatives (more than ever), 4) In conditionals (if you ever), 5) With superlatives (best ever), 6) For emphasis in questions (Why ever...?). It generally means 'at any time' or 'at all times'.
rules.explanation_es: 'Ever' tiene múltiples usos en inglés: 1) En preguntas sobre experiencias de vida (¿Alguna vez...?), 2) Con negativos para énfasis (nunca jamás), 3) En comparativos (más que nunca), 4) En condicionales (si alguna vez), 5) Con superlativos (el mejor de todos los tiempos), 6) Para énfasis en preguntas (¿Por qué diablos...?).
examples: Experience (Present Perfect): Have you ever been to Paris?
Experience (Past): Did you ever meet him?
Negative emphasis: Don't ever do that again!
Double negative emphasis: I will never ever forget this.
Comparative: She's happier than ever before.
Comparative: It's more important than ever.
Conditional: If you ever need help, call me.
Conditional: Whenever you want, come visit us.
Superlative: This is the best pizza ever!
Superlative: He's the fastest runner ever.
Emphasis question: Why ever did you say that?
Emphasis question: What ever were you thinking?
Common phrases: forever (for all time), whatever (anything), whoever (anyone), wherever (any place), however (in any way)
rules.note: 💡 Common phrase: 'Have you ever...?' is used with Present Perfect to ask about life experiences. 'Ever' with negatives creates strong emphasis. 'Whatever', 'whoever', 'wherever', 'whenever', 'however' are compound words formed with 'ever'.

rules.explanation_en: SAY is used to express words or statements. It does NOT take an indirect object directly after it. TELL requires a person (indirect object) immediately after it. SAY focuses on the words spoken; TELL focuses on the person receiving the information.
rules.explanation_es: SAY se usa para expresar palabras o declaraciones. NO lleva objeto indirecto directamente después. TELL requiere una persona (objeto indirecto) inmediatamente después. SAY se enfoca en las palabras; TELL se enfoca en la persona que recibe la información.
examples: ✅ She said (that) she was tired. (correct - no person after 'said')
✅ She said something to me. (correct - 'to me' comes after)
❌ She said me she was tired. (WRONG - can't put person directly after 'said')
✅ She told me (that) she was tired. (correct - person after 'told')
❌ She told she was tired. (WRONG - missing person after 'told')
✅ He said, 'I'm leaving.' (direct quote)
✅ He told me he was leaving. (reported speech)
✅ Tell me the truth. (command - person required)
❌ Say me the truth. (WRONG - can't use 'say' this way)
✅ What did you say? (asking about words)
✅ What did you tell him? (asking what info was given to him)
✅ He said hello to everyone. (say + to)
✅ He told everyone to sit down. (tell + person + infinitive)
rules.note: ⚠️ KEY RULE: SAY + (that) + statement OR say + something + TO + someone. TELL + person + (that) + statement. You CANNOT say 'say me' but you CAN say 'tell me'. Think: TELL = TELL someone (person always needed).

rules.rule_name: When to Use 'TO' Before Object Pronouns - Direct vs Indirect Objects
rules.structure: VERB + OBJECT (direct) vs VERB + TO + OBJECT (indirect)
rules.explanation_en: Some verbs take a DIRECT object (no 'to' needed), while others take an INDIRECT object (requires 'to'). Direct objects receive the action directly. Indirect objects are affected by the action but need 'to' to show the relationship.
rules.explanation_es: Algunos verbos toman objeto DIRECTO (no necesita 'to'), mientras otros toman objeto INDIRECTO (requiere 'to'). Los objetos directos reciben la acción directamente. Los objetos indirectos son afectados por la acción pero necesitan 'to' para mostrar la relación.

direct_object_verbs.explanation: These verbs take the object DIRECTLY (NO 'to' needed)
examples: ✅ I love you. (NOT: I love to you)
✅ She knows him. (NOT: She knows to him)
✅ He helped me. (NOT: He helped to me)
✅ They called us. (NOT: They called to us)
✅ I'll meet you there. (NOT: I'll meet to you)
✅ She thanked him. (NOT: She thanked to him)
✅ Can you forgive me? (NOT: Can you forgive to me)

indirect_object_verbs.explanation: These verbs need 'TO' before the object
examples: ✅ What happened to him? (NOT: What happened him?)
✅ Listen to me! (NOT: Listen me!)
✅ She spoke to them. (NOT: She spoke them)
✅ He explained it to us. (NOT: He explained it us)
✅ I apologized to her. (NOT: I apologized her)
✅ This belongs to me. (NOT: This belongs me)
✅ Please respond to him. (NOT: Please respond him)

special_cases.explanation: Some verbs can work BOTH ways depending on structure
examples: GIVE: I gave him a book. (indirect object first) OR I gave a book to him. (direct object first)
SEND: She sent me a message. OR She sent a message to me.
SHOW: He showed us the way. OR He showed the way to us.
TEACH: I'll teach you English. OR I'll teach English to you.
WRITE: I wrote her a letter. OR I wrote a letter to her.

memory_trick.title: 💡 How to Remember
tips: If you can ask 'WHAT happened?' → use TO (What happened TO him?)
If you can ask 'WHO do you love?' → NO to (I love YOU, not 'to you')
Communication verbs often need TO: speak TO, talk TO, listen TO
Emotion/feeling verbs usually DON'T need TO: love, hate, like, know

common_mistakes: ❌ What happened him? → ✅ What happened to him?
❌ I love to you. → ✅ I love you.
❌ She knows to him. → ✅ She knows him.
❌ Speak me in English. → ✅ Speak to me in English.
❌ It belongs me. → ✅ It belongs to me.
❌ I apologized him. → ✅ I apologized to him.
